name: Gambrel barn silhouette
description: A wide rectangular barn capped by the iconic gambrel (Dutch-style) roof: a two-pitch profile whose lower slopes are nearly vertical and whose upper slopes are shallower, meeting at a central ridge. This double-pitch geometry pushes the eaves outward and maximizes usable volume under the roof, traditionally a hay loft above a ground-floor animal stable. The footprint is markedly elongated (length roughly 1.4 to 1.8 times the width), and the roof ridge runs along the long axis. A large central door — wide enough for carts, livestock, or hay wagons — is placed on one gable end under the peak. Common in American and Dutch colonial farmsteads, this silhouette also adapts to rustic outbuildings and Victorian-era farm complexes.
placement_rules: Long axis of the footprint determines roof ridge orientation; the gambrel profile is read on the short (gable) ends.
Aspect ratio (length:width) should stay between 1.4 and 1.8 — narrower reads as a square shed, wider as an over-stretched longhouse.
Lower roof pitch is steep (near-vertical, around 60-75 degrees) and rises about 60% of the total roof height; upper pitch is shallow (around 20-35 degrees) and forms the remainder up to the ridge.
Place a single large central door on one gable end, framed under the lower-pitch break-line, sized to admit carts or hay wagons (3-4 blocks wide, 4-5 blocks tall).
Distribute small loft openings or hay doors high on the gable ends, centered under the upper peak.
Wall height is typically 1 storey (4-6 blocks) before the lower roof pitch begins; loft volume occupies the gambrel interior above.

rustic.notes: Classic American red-barn ancestor in weathered spruce; exposed corner posts; wide hay-door on gable end.

victorian.notes: Painted barn-red cladding with white trim suggestion via accent contrast; ornamental cupola or weathervane optional on the ridge.
